description: A preservative that has been linked to skin sensitisation and allergic reactions. The EU has restricted its use in leave-on cosmetics.
A strong preservative banned in leave-on cosmetics in the EU. Highly sensitising and linked to severe allergic reactions.
A synthetic antioxidant also used in food. Possible endocrine disruptor and skin sensitiser at higher concentrations.
A chemical sunscreen filter that penetrates the skin and has been detected in blood and breast milk. Linked to hormonal disruption and coral reef damage.
A known human carcinogen used as a preservative. Banned or restricted in many countries. Can cause skin sensitisation and irritation.
A formaldehyde-releasing preservative. As it breaks down it releases formaldehyde, a known carcinogen. Common cause of allergic reactions.
A formaldehyde-releasing preservative and one of the most common causes of allergic contact dermatitis from cosmetics.
A phthalate plasticiser used in nail polish. Banned in the EU. Strong endocrine disruptor linked to reproductive harm.
A lead compound used as a hair colorant. Lead is a toxic heavy metal with no safe level of exposure. Banned in the EU and Canada.
A byproduct of coal processing used in anti-dandruff shampoos. Known human carcinogen. Banned in EU cosmetics.

A catch-all term that can hide hundreds of undisclosed chemicals. Common cause of skin sensitisation and allergic reactions. Not all fragrances are harmful — but the lack of transparency is concerning.
A catch-all term that can hide hundreds of undisclosed chemicals. Common cause of skin sensitisation and allergic reactions.
A strong cleansing agent that can strip natural oils from skin and hair. Can cause irritation with frequent use. Not a carcinogen despite claims — but can be harsh for sensitive skin.
A milder version of SLS, widely used in shampoos and body wash. Generally considered safe but can be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane during manufacturing.
The most widely used paraben preservative. Weak oestrogen-mimicking activity detected in lab studies. EU considers safe at current levels but many brands have reformulated away from parabens.
A paraben preservative with stronger oestrogenic activity than methylparaben. EU has restricted its use. Avoid during pregnancy.
A paraben with significant oestrogenic activity. Restricted in EU baby products. Higher concern than methyl or ethylparaben.
An emulsifier that can be contaminated with ethylene oxide and 1,4-dioxane — both potential carcinogens — during manufacturing. Generally safe but quality of manufacture matters.
A widely used cosmetic ingredient that can be contaminated with ethylene oxide and 1,4-dioxane during manufacturing. On intact skin generally safe — concern is with damaged skin.
Can react with nitrites in formulas to form carcinogenic nitrosamines. EU restricts its use. Generally safe in well-formulated products but worth monitoring.
A widely used preservative considered a safer alternative to parabens. Generally well tolerated but can cause reactions in sensitive individuals. EU restricts concentration to 1%.
A widely used chemical sunscreen. Penetrates skin and has been detected in blood. Some evidence of weak hormonal activity. Harmful to coral reefs.
Generally safe in cosmetics. Concern is specifically with crystalline silica in powder products that could be inhaled — amorphous silica used in cosmetics is considered safe.
Standard alcohol used as a solvent and preservative. In high concentrations can be drying and disrupt the skin barrier. Fine in small amounts. Avoid in dry/sensitive skin products.
Purified water — the most common cosmetic ingredient. Completely safe.
Purified water — the most common cosmetic ingredient. Completely safe.
A naturally derived humectant that draws moisture to the skin. One of the safest and most effective skincare ingredients. Can be plant or animal derived.
A form of Vitamin B3 with multiple skin benefits — reduces pores, brightens skin tone, strengthens skin barrier. Very well tolerated. One of the most evidence-backed skincare ingredients.
A Vitamin A derivative with strong anti-ageing evidence. Can cause initial irritation. Avoid during pregnancy — high doses of Vitamin A are linked to birth defects.
A naturally occurring molecule in the body that holds up to 1000x its weight in water. Excellent hydrating ingredient. Very well tolerated by all skin types.
A natural antioxidant and skin conditioner identical to Vitamin E. Protects skin from oxidative damage. Well tolerated — rare allergic reactions possible.
Pure Vitamin C — a powerful antioxidant that brightens skin and stimulates collagen production. Unstable in formulas but highly effective. Safe for all skin types.
Extract from the aloe vera plant with soothing and hydrating properties. Well tolerated. Rare allergic reactions in people sensitive to the Liliaceae plant family.
A silicone polymer that creates a smooth feel and protects skin. Not absorbed by the skin. Environmental concern as it is not biodegradable.
Despite the name, not a drying alcohol. A fatty alcohol derived from palm or coconut oil that conditions and thickens formulas. Safe for all skin types.
The sodium salt of hyaluronic acid. Smaller molecule than hyaluronic acid so may penetrate slightly deeper. Excellent hydrating ingredient. Completely safe.
A provitamin of B5 that converts to pantothenic acid in the skin. Excellent moisturiser and wound healer. Very safe — widely used in baby products.
A mineral sunscreen that sits on skin and reflects UV rays. Does not penetrate skin significantly. Safe for sensitive skin, children, and pregnancy. The safest sunscreen filter available.
concerns: nano particle form — some concerns about inhalation in spray form

description: A mineral pigment and sunscreen filter. Safe in cream/lotion form. Some concern with nano-particle spray products where inhalation is possible.
A naturally occurring acid found in citrus fruits. Used to adjust pH in cosmetic formulas. Safe at concentrations used in cosmetics.
A natural fat extracted from the shea tree nut. Rich emollient with anti-inflammatory properties. Safe for most skin types — avoid if allergic to tree nuts.
A beta-hydroxy acid that exfoliates inside pores. Excellent for acne-prone skin. Avoid during pregnancy at high concentrations.
concerns: avoid during pregnancy at high concentrations

description: An alpha-hydroxy acid that exfoliates and hydrates. Gentler than glycolic acid. Use SPF when using AHAs as they increase sun sensitivity.
The smallest AHA molecule, allowing deep penetration. Effective exfoliant and anti-ageing ingredient. Can cause irritation at high concentrations. Always use SPF.
A fatty alcohol blend used as an emollient and thickener. Not a drying alcohol — conditions and softens skin. Very well tolerated.
A naturally occurring fatty acid found in animal and vegetable fats. Used as an emulsifier and thickener. Very safe.
Derived from coconut oil. A lightweight emollient that softens skin without a greasy feel. Very well tolerated by all skin types.
A stable, hydrogenated form of squalene. Naturally present in human sebum. Excellent lightweight moisturiser. Modern squalane is typically plant-derived (olive or sugarcane).
A lipid naturally found in the skin's barrier. Helps repair and strengthen the moisture barrier. Excellent for dry and sensitive skin. Completely safe.
A soothing and moisturising ingredient that promotes cell regeneration. Found naturally in comfrey root. Very well tolerated.
Common table salt used as a thickener in shampoos and body washes. Completely safe in cosmetic use.
A synthetic humectant and solvent. Can occasionally cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals. Generally recognised as safe by the FDA.
A lightweight humectant and solvent that helps other ingredients penetrate the skin. Very well tolerated.
A natural polysaccharide produced by bacterial fermentation. Safe and effective thickener used in many cosmetic and food products.
A synthetic polymer used to thicken and stabilise cosmetic formulations. Very well tolerated. Found in gels, creams, and serums.
A chelating agent that binds to metal ions to improve product stability. Not significantly absorbed through skin. Environmental persistence is a concern.
A stabilised form of Vitamin E. Converts to tocopherol on the skin. Provides antioxidant protection and moisturisation.
Used in tiny amounts to adjust pH. In finished cosmetic products the concentration is negligible and safe. Essential in soap making.
A mild surfactant derived from coconut oil. Gentler than SLS. Can occasionally cause contact dermatitis due to manufacturing impurities (amidoamine).
An emollient that improves product spreadability. Moderately comedogenic — may clog pores in acne-prone individuals.
A volatile silicone that evaporates after application, leaving a silky feel. EU has restricted its use in wash-off products due to environmental persistence.
An ethoxylated fatty alcohol used as an emulsifier. Can contain trace amounts of ethylene oxide from manufacturing.
A naturally derived emulsifier and emollient made from glycerin and stearic acid. Very well tolerated. Used in creams and lotions.
A highly refined petroleum-derived oil. Creates an occlusive barrier on skin. Cosmetic-grade mineral oil is highly purified and considered safe. Does not penetrate skin.
The INCI name for mineral oil in European cosmetics. Highly refined and considered safe for cosmetic use. Creates a protective barrier on skin.
A highly effective occlusive moisturiser. Cosmetic-grade petrolatum is refined and considered safe. Forms a protective barrier that prevents moisture loss.
Natural wax produced by bees. Used as an emollient and thickener. Very well tolerated. Not vegan.
A natural wax secreted by sheep's skin. Excellent emollient but can cause allergic reactions in some people. Not vegan.
A natural plant oil rich in essential fatty acids and vitamin A. Promotes skin regeneration and reduces hyperpigmentation. Well tolerated.
A natural oil from the argan tree. Rich in vitamin E and fatty acids. Excellent for hair and skin. Non-comedogenic.
Natural coconut oil with moisturising properties. Moderately comedogenic — may not suit acne-prone facial skin. Excellent for body and hair.
A lightweight plant oil rich in linoleic acid. Helps strengthen the skin barrier. Non-comedogenic and well tolerated.
A gentle plant oil rich in vitamins A and E. Excellent emollient for dry skin. Avoid if allergic to tree nuts.
Technically a liquid wax, not an oil. Very similar to human sebum so absorbs well. Non-comedogenic and suitable for all skin types.
Rich in polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Well-studied skincare ingredient with multiple benefits.
A plant extract used in traditional medicine for centuries. Promotes wound healing and reduces inflammation. Popular in K-beauty products.
A common preservative also used in food. Generally well tolerated in cosmetics. Can cause mild irritation in very sensitive individuals.
A mild preservative derived from sorbic acid. One of the gentlest preservatives available. Widely used in natural and organic cosmetics.
A preservative booster derived from glycerin. Enhances the efficacy of other preservatives, allowing lower concentrations. Very well tolerated.
A synthetic polymer used to thicken and stabilise formulations. Not absorbed through skin. Well tolerated.
A formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Releases small amounts of formaldehyde over time. Can cause contact dermatitis.
A formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Releases more formaldehyde than imidazolidinyl urea. Common cause of allergic contact dermatitis.
A preservative that can release formaldehyde and form nitrosamines. EU permits its use at low concentrations. Avoid in products with amines.
A chemical UV filter. Less concerning than oxybenzone but still a synthetic UV absorber with some evidence of photoallergic reactions.
A paraben preservative with weak oestrogenic activity. Generally considered lower risk than propyl or butylparaben.
A paraben with oestrogenic activity similar to butylparaben. Restricted in EU baby products. Higher concern among parabens.
A chemical sunscreen filter that can penetrate skin. EU Scientific Committee has recommended reducing maximum concentration. Some evidence of endocrine activity.
A chemical UV filter that stabilises other sunscreen ingredients. Can degrade into benzophenone over time. Some photoallergic reactions reported.
Ethanol that has been denatured to prevent ingestion. Can be drying at high concentrations. Acceptable in small amounts but avoid as a main ingredient in leave-on products for dry skin.
A strong solvent and antimicrobial. Very drying to skin. Used in some toners and aftershaves. Avoid in products for dry or sensitive skin.
A common preservative also used in food. Generally well tolerated in cosmetics.
Natural olive oil with moisturising and antioxidant properties. Rich in squalene and vitamin E. Moderately comedogenic.
A thick plant oil popular in lip products and hair care. Provides shine and moisture. Non-comedogenic despite its thick texture.
A rich plant oil high in oleic acid and vitamins A, D, and E. Excellent for dry skin. Deeply nourishing.
Derived from chamomile. A potent anti-inflammatory and soothing ingredient. Helps calm irritated skin. Very well tolerated.
A stable, water-soluble derivative of Vitamin C. Less potent than pure ascorbic acid but more stable in formulations. Gentle and effective.
A naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid. Treats acne, rosacea, and hyperpigmentation. One of the few actives considered safe during pregnancy.
A powerful antibacterial agent for acne treatment. Can cause dryness, peeling, and bleach fabrics. Start with low concentrations.
Colour Index number for titanium dioxide when used as a pigment in makeup. Safe in cream/lotion form.
concerns: inhalation risk in loose powder form

description: A naturally derived mineral pigment. Provides red/brown colour in foundations and blushes. Safe and well tolerated.
A naturally derived mineral pigment. Provides yellow tones in foundations and concealers. Safe and well tolerated.
A naturally derived mineral pigment. Provides dark tones in eyeliners, mascara, and foundations. Safe and well tolerated.
A naturally occurring mineral that provides shimmer and sparkle in cosmetics. Safe for skin. Some ethical concerns about mining practices.
A natural clay mineral used in masks, foundations, and powders. Absorbs excess oil without over-drying. Gentle enough for sensitive skin.
A natural volcanic clay with excellent oil-absorbing properties. Popular in face masks. Can be drying if used too frequently.
A naturally occurring mineral used as a thickener and stabiliser. Not absorbed through skin. Well tolerated.
The sodium salt of pyrrolidone carboxylic acid, a component of the skin's natural moisturising factor. Excellent humectant. Completely safe.
A natural component of the skin's moisture factor. At low concentrations (2-10%) it hydrates; at higher concentrations (20-40%) it exfoliates. Very effective for dry skin.
A plant-derived polymer used to thicken water-based formulations. Creates a smooth, non-tacky gel texture. Well tolerated.
